data.title: 3 Things - Tariffs, Of Course
data.description:
Here are three things I am thinking about that are really only one thing, of course.

1) Why Tariffs are Bad

I got back into making YouTube videos this weekend. This time I touched on tariffs, how they work, the many myths around them, and why they’re bad.

This whole thing really blows my mind because this was never a controversial topic in economics. It might be one of the few things that Milton Friedman and Paul Krugman would have agreed on completely, despite their vast political differences. So it’s disappointing to see so much misinformation about it.

In this video, I discuss how tariffs work and why 95%+ of economists think tariffs are bad. I try to clarify some of the confusion surrounding this topic and the bad narratives swirling. I hope you find it helpful. And yes, I apologize in advance if this sounds political. I can assure you it’s not. This is just one of the most empirically supported concepts in all of macroeconomics, and it’s frustrating that, after 80 years of putting this idea to rest, it’s suddenly being revived. I saw someone online say that tariffs arise once every 80 years because it takes an entire generation to forget how terrible they are. I hope that’s not true, but it sure looks like it’s the case.

2) A Little Game Theory

One thing that the administration doesn’t appear to have done here is any game theory around this topic. I think they thought that they could bully the rest of the world because we’re the biggest kid in the schoolyard. But as it turns out, the other kids aren’t being so easily bullied, and many of them are actually fighting back and hitting us pretty hard. And I am not sure how the administration can back down from this view now. In fact, they appear to be steadfast in their view that this is going to work in the long run and that we all just need to endure a bit of short-term pain.

Anyhow, I am trying to assess the various ways this could all play out. My baseline view on the sheer size of these tariffs is that they’re almost certainly recessionary if they remain in place. So the question is, how long will they remain in place and what would it take to get the White House to realize this is a bad policy? Well, it’s looking like a stock market decline isn’t going to convince them. And that’s probably the right call. The stock market overreacts sometimes. But what if the stock market stabilizes from here? That’s a good thing, right? I worry that it might embolden the White House and give them confidence that this isn’t really a big deal. And what could happen in that scenario is that global trade slows meaningfully, business uncertainty lingers, and that ultimately starts this long slow bleed into the real economy over time. We start getting weaker and weaker employment reports, the unemployment rate jumps to 5%, 6%, 7%, and then it becomes abundantly clear that this isn’t just hurting a bunch of rich stockholders but is actually hurting the people the White House wants to help with this policy.

I sure hope I am wrong about that. And I hope we can all move past this self-inflicted wound sooner than later, because the longer this lasts, the worse the economy will get.

3) How About that Countercyclical Indexing?

Okay, this isn’t really about tariffs, but it is related to the way tariffs have impacted my portfolio.

The Defined Duration strategy is a bucketing style strategy that implements a financial planning-based asset-liability matching strategy. In short, it tries to quantify your expenses and liabilities over certain time horizons and then matches them to specific assets. I came up with it during Covid because the Covid crash scared me, primarily because I had an infant which made me realize I couldn’t afford to be as risky as I was being. After all, I had dependents now and their reliance on me created more short-term and long-term cash flow needs than I could predict. I needed more certainty in my portfolio, and after having gone through the last few weeks, I can’t tell you how powerful I think this approach actually is. I feel more behaviorally resilient to stock downturns than I ever have because I have near certainty over my ability to meet short-term expenses and liabilities thanks to the shorter-duration buckets.

It’s a very cool thing to implement and then live through when compared to the way I used to allocate assets in one big random blob of diversified things. Using that approach I’d think of my portfolio as one bucket, and as long as it was “diversified”, I’d convince myself that it was okay. But it gave me no actual sense of certainty over how my assets actually related to my spending needs, and that is really all anyone cares about. Quantifying the durations of assets and then matching them to my financial plan allows me to look at my asset allocation and actually understand how certain pieces are serving my needs over different time horizons, which helps me stay the course when the longer-duration volatile buckets get crazy.

In theory, you could bucket it out across a million time horizons, but for the sake of simplicity, I like to bucket this out in 5 specific buckets (0-2 years, 2-5 years, 5-15 years, 15+ years & insurance if necessary). The short-term bucket is, ironically, the most active because it’s typically T-Bills maturing regularly. The next rung is intermediate bonds. But the 5-15 year bucket is the one I find the most intriguing because it is where our multi-asset instruments make the most sense. That is, something like 60/40 or any diversified stock/bond strategy ends up with a blended duration of something close to 10 years on average. 60/40 is about 12 years, and so, the reason I find this one the most intriguing is because it’s the one that is closest to what the average investor holds and also requires the most sophistication. After all, the bond buckets are just bonds of a certain duration, and the 15+ year bucket is usually just stocks of some mix. But the reason the 5-15 year bucket is interesting is because it’s the one that’s most behaviorally difficult. After all, that weird time horizon is the one that’s kind of a gray area for most of us. You can’t be super short with it, you can’t be super long with it, and you don’t want to be only bonds with it (because you have too much inflation risk over that time horizon).

Anyhow, I choose to use my Countercyclical Indexing strategy in this bucket because the countercyclical approach helps to throttle the risk in the stock piece a bit. In other words, if you defaulted to a 12-year duration via the 60/40, then you end up with an uncomfortably long duration because the stock piece dominates the volatility of the portfolio. If you want more certainty, you have to tilt the duration shorter, and the only way to do that is to either hold something very conservative like 40/60 or use an instrument that can tax-efficiently move that 40/60 inside of itself without tax implications, as the risks evolve over time.

And boy, has that worked well this year. Of course, you get less upside on the way up, but you get more protection in the moments when you really need it. And it’s times like these where the behavioral robustness of your portfolio becomes most important. And that is, after all, why John Bogle used a countercyclical approach at times. It’s just a super-sensible behavioral tool to help you stay the course in a multi-asset portfolio.

I live for these moments, because these are the times where behavior really becomes magnified. I hope that some of my writing over the years about these time-based approaches has helped you build a little bit more of a robust portfolio so that moments like these don’t keep you up all night.

Thanks for reading, and as always, stay disciplined.

NB – Bonus Thing: The Miran Paper

A number of people have emailed me asking me about the Stephen Miran (Chair of the White House Economic Council) paper that is the basis for many of the arguments in favor of tariffs. It’s here if you want to read it. I won’t critique the paper directly, and I’ve interacted with Stephen in the past. I like him and he’s always struck me as a sharp guy. But one thing I will say about all of this more broadly is that I just don’t understand why we’re using this particular tool to combat this particular problem.

In the video above, I touch on why the current account balance isn’t a problem (it’s just not an issue to be sending $1 abroad when you make $30 domestically and have the biggest asset base in human history to support that income statement). People are always talking about “global trade imbalances”, but the USA does not have an imbalance at all. If anything, the wealth is imbalanced in favor of the USA, and that $1 outflow doesn’t correctly reflect the sustainability of the balance sheet. I also touched on why manufacturing employment is a problem that we’re not going to fix (automation is going to kill what’s left of it in the future). But if you really wanted to boost domestic manufacturing, then why wouldn’t you just lean into more domestic manufacturing investment directly? We could easily cut spending elsewhere and retarget government spending in manufacturing. That would be a much more direct and reasonable way to combat this problem instead of destroying trade relationships and taxing corporations via tariffs. So, I just don’t even see the logic here. There are so many better ways to attack this issue than the way they’re going about this... Alright. Hopefully I’ve said my piece on this issue.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ArcelorMittal's Big Buyback Plan Will Enrich Shareholders
data.description:
As a value investor, I always enjoy digging into companies from a fundamental perspective, especially when those companies end up being cheap from a valuation standpoint. The steel production industry is known for low multiples, and that's because of volatility in the space that can cause wild swings in revenue and profits. However, some of the companies are cheaper than others. One prospect that I believe deserves attention is ArcelorMittal (NYSE:MT), a business that boasts a market capitalization as of this time of $19.16 billion.

Back in March of last year, I wrote an article about how the company had agreed to acquire a 28.4% stake in Vallourec (OTCPK:VLOUF) (OTCPK:VLOWY) in a deal valued at $1.04 billion. I viewed this as a great purchase for the company based on the figures involved at the time. Ultimately, I ended up rating ArcelorMittal a ‘buy’ based on that transaction and how the company looked from a valuation standpoint. Since then, shares have pulled back by 7.7%, which is worse than the 2.2% decline that the S&P 500 has been down. Certainly, we have seen some weakness during this time. But even with that factored in, and a large amount of uncertainty regarding the tariff situation and the impact it will have on the economy, I still think that the company is a solid prospect.

Management seems to think so as well. I say this because, on April 2nd of this year, management announced that the company had completed the purchase of 85 million shares under its old share buyback program. The new program extends from this year through May 2030 and will be done in tranches, with the first of these for up to 10 million shares. We don't know the exact number of shares management is planning to buy. However, management did specify that they intend to allocate at least 50% of all post dividend free cash flow generated by the company toward these purchases. Considering how much cash the company generates, this is a big bet on future success. And with how cheap shares currently are, it could be one that pays big dividends for shareholders in the long run.

A look at ArcelorMittal

Operationally speaking, ArcelorMittal is a major integrated steel and mining company. It currently serves as the largest steel producer in Europe and is a major producer in the Americas. The firm also has operations in parts of Asia, including in India through a joint venture that it has called AMNS India. All told, the company has steel making operations in 15 different countries. The 125,416 employees that it ended 2024 with are integral and running its 36 integrated and mini mill steel making facilities spread across these nations.

In 2024, the company was successful in producing 57.9 million tons of crude steel. Its iron ore production that year was 42.4 million tons. These numbers compared to the 58.1 million and 42 million, respectively, reported one year earlier. Total steel shipments for last year came out to 54.3 million tons. That was down a bit from the 55.6 million tons reported the previous year. This, unfortunately, was part of the reason why revenue for the company declined year over year. But before we get to those figures, it would be helpful to understand a bit more about the company.

According to management, the business produces a variety of steel products. This includes high quality finished and semi-finished steel offerings. It specializes in producing flat products like sheet and plate products, as well as long products such as bars, rods, and structural shapes. The firm produces pipes and tubes that are used by a large number of end markets. And it sells all of these goods to customers spread across 129 different countries.

Even though the company has a wide range of products that it sells, it is worth noting that some are more important than others. As an example, 20% of the company's revenue comes from sales to the construction industry. When you consider how much steel is needed for bridges, high-rise buildings, and more, this makes a lot of sense. Another 17% of revenue comes from the automotive and mobility end markets. This is also logical. The largest chunk of revenue, about 23%, comes from its distribution operations. Through these, the company sells steel products in bulk to service centers, re-rollers, and other customers. The end goal there is for those companies to process them further or to resell them as is. You also have 14% of revenue coming from what management calls ‘primary transformation’, which includes semi-finished products that can be used in not only the automotive and construction space, but also in the production of appliances and other steel-oriented goods.

As I mentioned earlier, the company did experience a decline in steel shipments from 2023 to 2024. This unfortunately pushed revenue down from $68.28 billion to $62.44 billion. In North America, revenue contracted from $12.98 billion to $11.90 billion. The decline in steel shipments from 10.56 million tons to 10.06 million tons was partially responsible for this. But also problematic was the fact that the company suffered from a decline in the average steel selling price. This ultimately fell from $1,024 per ton to $985 per ton. According to management, the decline in steel shipments was driven mostly by an illegal blockade at the company's Mexican operations that impacted results throughout the year.

This was not the only area of pain for the company. Take, for instance, Brazil. Revenue there dropped from $13.16 billion to $12.40 billion. This came even as steel shipments grew from 13.68 million tons to 14.08 million tons. Over the same window of time, pricing dropped from $939 per ton to $816 per ton. Management attributed the rise in production to the fact that the company, in March 2023, ended up consolidating its ArcelorMittal Pecem operations. Without this, it appears as though production would have been more or less flat.

As I mentioned earlier in this article, ArcelorMittal is a major producer of steel in Europe. In fact, it's the largest. Last year, the company generated $29.95 billion from the continent. That represents a decline from the $31.70 billion reported the previous year. This was in spite of the fact that total steel shipments rose from 27.56 million tons to 28.66 million tons as production rose following blast furnace outages in both Spain and France in 2023. Unfortunately, this was more than offset by a drop in the average steel selling price from $995 per ton to $910 per ton.

The company also has an entire segment called Sustainable Solutions. This part of the company is really a collection of businesses such as those involving renewable projects, construction activities, and more. For years now, this segment has been experiencing a decline in revenue also. From 2023 to 2024, revenue dropped from $11.47 billion to $10.72 billion. This decline of 6.5% was because of lower activity levels and prices that management attributed to weaker end market conditions. Lastly, we have its Mining business, which, as its name denotes, is involved in the firm's mining operations. In 2024, this part of the company produced 27.9 million tons of iron ore. It shipped 26.4 million tons of it. These figures compare to the 26 million and 26.4 million, respectively, reported one year earlier. But because of an 8.3% drop in iron ore reference prices, revenue for this segment contracted from $3.08 billion to $2.66 billion.

In this kind of industry, whenever revenue falls, you can expect profits and cash flows to generally worsen. While net income bucked the trend, rising from $1.02 billion to $1.38 billion, this was really only because of one-time things. As an example, in 2023, net profits were negatively affected to the tune of $2.43 billion thanks to the company's decision to dispose of its operations in Kazakhstan. It also incurred a $1.41 billion impairment on certain investments and associates, joint ventures, and more.

In 2024, the firm reported $572 million in one-off tax charges, $216 million in exceptional items, $116 million in other miscellaneous impairments, and $83 million involving mark to market losses associated with its aforementioned acquisition of its 28.4% stake in Vallourec. All told, the company's adjusted net profits fell from $4.87 billion to $2.33 billion. Other profitability metrics contracted as well. Operating cash flow dropped from $7.65 billion to $4.85 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a decline from $5.92 billion to $5.24 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business fell from $8.74 billion to $7.05 billion.

Using the historical figures for 2023 and 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. I always love seeing a company trade in the low single digit range. In addition to being cheap on an absolute basis, shares are also cheap compared to other similar enterprises. In the table below, I compared it with five such businesses. On both a price to operating cash flow basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, you can see that only one of the five companies I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
ArcelorMittal 3.7 3.7
Steel Dynamics (STLD) 9.4 7.5
POSCO Holdings (PKX) 3.2 3.3
Nucor (NUE) 6.3 6.1
Reliance (RS) 10.4 9.6
United States Steel Corporation (X) 12.3 8.4

While shares of the company are attractively priced, there are some other items that we should discuss. At the forefront of everybody's mind would be the impact that tariffs might have on the business. Steel importers, for instance, are sure to be negatively affected if tariffs continue as they have been recently. But this might not be the worst thing for ArcelorMittal. In early February, when the company announced financial results for the final quarter of 2024, it specified that when tariffs were imposed in 2018 and 2019, tariffs that were on Canadian and Mexican steel, the cost to the business was roughly $100 million each quarter. However, they claimed that the revenue increase that they were able to achieve by hiking prices more than offset this.

Unfortunately, this was before the global tariff on steel and aluminum production was announced, so we don't know what the picture will look like today. We do know that the company produces about 38% of its steel in the Americas. But this includes all parts of the Americas that it operates in, not just the US. We do know that, in February, the company announced plans to construct an advanced manufacturing facility in Alabama that would be capable of producing up to 150,000 tons of electrical steel each year. Management is specifically targeting the automotive and industrial and markets for this. However, this would be a long-term initiative that will require net capital expenditures of $0.9 billion and that won't be in operation at all until the second-half of 2027.

This is just part of the projects that the company is engaged in for the purpose of achieving a rise in profitability over the next few years. In fact, the goal is to grow structural EBITDA by $1.9 billion by the end of 2027. This is on top of the $0.2 billion the business should generate thanks to its minority acquisition of Vallourec. Management designates these improvements as organic strategic growth initiatives. About 33% of the EBITDA growth should involve North America. However, other major areas of focus include India, Liberia, Brazil, and Europe. Of course, these types of investments don't come cheap. For context, capital expenditures in 2024 came out to $4.41 billion. And over the last four years combined, capital spending amounted to $15.49 billion. For 2025, management expects to spend between $4.5 billion and $5 billion on capital projects, with $1.4 billion to $1.5 billion of that involving strategic growth projects. $2.8 billion to $3.1 billion will involve maintenance capital spending, while the remaining $0.3 billion to $0.4 billion will involve decarbonization initiatives.

To be honest with you, I am really concerned about the automotive end market. According to Cox Automotive, the tariffs applied by the US should cause the average price of a new car imported into the country to rise by $6,000. Because 50% to 60% of all content within new vehicles produced domestically ultimately comes from outside of the country, they also expect the price of domestically produced vehicles to rise by $3,000. This could cause, according to some estimates, a reduction in new vehicles sold in the country of around 3 million units. That would mean an 18.8% reduction in overall sales from the 16 million produced last year to the expected 3 million that could be produced moving forward. Naturally, this would have a negative impact on the company's revenue and profits. If you add on top of this other industries that could be negatively affected by tariffs, such as the construction space that will see input costs rise considerably, then it wouldn't be unthinkable that times could be a bit difficult in the near term.

Takeaway

Despite broader concerns for the company and the industry, I do believe that ArcelorMittal makes for an attractive opportunity. The company is making interesting investments and, while revenue and profits and cash flows have all declined over the last couple of years, shares are trading at attractive levels. This is true on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. At the end of the day, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘buy’ for now. And frankly, while I tend to prefer that capital be allocated toward growth projects, considering how cheap shares are at the moment, I believe that management's decision to put a meaningful amount of capital each year toward share buybacks makes a lot of sense.

data.title: Tesla Is Our Highest Conviction Long In The Tariff Fallout (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) valuations have depreciated by more than 50% since the Trump rally peaked in mid-December. The combination of slowing auto sales and head-on exposure to tariff implications have been a one-two punch to both the stock’s valuation and investor confidence in Tesla’s autonomous mobility and robotics future.

However, Tesla’s fundamental prospects – even amidst a dour backdrop blighted by tariff uncertainties, demand risks and concerns about “Elon distraction” – should warrant a higher valuation from current levels. In the face of elevated market uncertainties, Tesla could finally be emerging as an attractive long-term opportunity.

1. History Might be Repeating Itself

While some cheered on the President Trump-Musk duo in the White House, others were reminded of the turmoil that the Tesla stock faced in 2022. The “Elon distraction” narrative was formed when Tesla CEO Elon Musk first announced his intentions to acquire Twitter (now “X”) in April 2022. By the time the transaction closed six months later, Tesla’s valuation had already plummeted about 40% from its 2022 peak. Coupled with repeated Tesla share-selling from Musk himself in the months that followed, the stock plunged another 30% by December 2022.

Similar concerns have resurfaced in recent months, with Musk’s DOGE involvement representing a contradiction to the kind of leadership that investors yearn for amidst Tesla’s ongoing auto sales declines. The stock’s lost more than 50% of its value since it last peaked in mid-December 2024.

But if history’s worth noting, Tesla might be on the verge of an inflection point.

After the stock bottomed in December 2022, it posted a steep recovery by doubling its valuation by mid-2023. During the short six-month period, Musk showed up to Tesla’s periodic earnings updates, and drove the company back on a streak of outperformance – both in auto deliveries and earnings growth. Musk also led Tesla in a pivotal shift to FSD (Full Self-Drive) v11 at the time, which was built on the company’s proprietary “single-stack” architecture. FSD v11 essentially laid the foundation for accelerated advancements in proceeding updates since, which also improved uptake.

It's likely that a similar inflection’s around the corner. Recall that Musk is currently a “special government employee” in the execution of ongoing DOGE tasks. Under the rules outlined by the Federal Labor Relations Authority, SGE personnel can only perform work for the Federal government for up to 130 days within any consecutive 365-day period. Considering Musk’s work in DOGE began at Trump’s inauguration on January 20, his time’s due for diversion back to Tesla by late May. This was further corroborated by the Trump administration’s recent comments that there are no intentions to keep Musk beyond the 130-day tenure designated for SGEs.

And Musk’s likely still got a few levers up his sleeve in which he could pull on to restore Tesla’s credibility. His impending return to Tesla will not only coincide with the ramp-up of the refreshed Model Y, but also the highly anticipated roll-out of the “more affordable model” in 2H25. The company’s also in the works of rolling out the general availability of FSD unsupervised in Texas by June, which should help restore the upward valuation re-rate currently earmarked for Tesla’s robotaxi ambitions.

2. The Stock’s Already Priced in the Worst and Then Some

Admittedly, the prospects of Tesla’s existing auto sales business look bleak. The slew of persistent delivery declines reported by local agencies across core operating regions like Germany, France, and China have undoubtedly been a setback for the stock. This was further validated by disappointing Q1 delivery figures disclosed by Tesla itself earlier this month.

But we’ve done some back-of-the-napkin calculations on what Tesla’s growth outlook would look like in the face of macro-driven demand risks – especially given current tariff and regulatory uncertainties borne by the broader EV industry. And the imminent downside risks have likely already been priced in at the stock’s current levels.

On average, economists are predicting U.S. GDP growth in 2025 to fall from earlier estimates of +2.2% y/y to now +1.7% y/y. This represents a 25% compression in U.S. GDP growth considering impending government spending cuts and import tariffs. And we’ve used this as a proxy to gauge the downside scenario of disruptions to Tesla.

Specifically, we’ve applied a 25% discount to previously discussed base case NTM growth projections at +13% y/y. The intuition of this downside scenario assumption is that the estimated 25% drop in GDP could trigger a similar downward shift in Tesla’s long-term growth curve.

An aggressive 25% discount to Tesla’s forward growth curve should be more than sufficient in capturing the totality of imminent company-specific and macroeconomic headwinds on its top- and bottom-line. This is consistent with the fact that neither Tesla management nor Musk himself have noted any changes to the company’s NTM growth outlook, despite the implementation of reciprocal tariffs. Since Musk’s a close alias with the Trump administration and an active commentator on X, it’s safe to assume that any material growth risks (i.e., greater than 25%) stemming from tariffs would’ve been shared already.

In addition to macroeconomic implications, the 25% growth discounts should also be more than sufficient in reflecting company-specific demand risks stemming from some consumers’ aversion to Musk’s political stance. This is consistent with the fact that Tesla China deliveries have already been steadily recovering since the completion of Giga Shanghai retooling for Model Y Juniper productions in late February. And although the recent slew of arson attacks across Tesla dealerships shed a bleak outlook for brand morale, there are silver-linings in place. The damages will likely be covered by insurance, and the lost vehicles could even mitigate Tesla’s exposure from inventory stockpiling in the transition to newer Model Ys.

By considering a 25% growth discount in the downside scenario, Tesla’s automotive and services and other sales are expected to grow at a 1% 10-year CAGR through 2035. This includes assumptions of a protracted trajectory of auto revenue declines through 2028. Given the incorporation of a lower-priced “affordable model” to Tesla’s line-up later this year to aid penetration into mass market opportunities, auto ASP will likely remain pressured within the foreseeable future and impact EV revenues.

By applying an 8x multiple on projected 2035 sales in the downside scenario, and discounted at a 10% WACC, Tesla’s auto and services and other segments are expected to yield an estimated intrinsic value of about $276 billion, or $79 per share. The valuation multiple applied to Tesla’s automotive and services and others business units is consistent with the average observed across its comparable megacap peers. Admittedly, this is a higher multiple compared to the average observed across legacy auto OEMs, but it adequately captures the valuation premium attributable to Tesla’s higher long-term growth outlook in EV sales.

A similar exercise was performed on Tesla’s energy generation and storage segment to determine its downside scenario valuation. After applying a 25% growth discount to previous NTM base case projections, the energy generation and storage segment is projected to expand at a 19% 10-year CAGR.

By applying a 5x multiple to projected 2035 sales in the downside scenario and discounted, the energy generation and storage business yields an estimated intrinsic value of about $197 billion, or $56 per share.

Taken together, all of Tesla’s existing automotive, services and other, and energy generation and storage businesses already account for about 60% of its current share price. And that’s yet to even include consideration of its robotaxi and FSD contributions, which are no longer as speculative as they once were, and shouldn’t be dismissed in gauging Tesla’s valuation.

3. It’d be a Mistake to Take Robotaxis and FSD as Wildcards

Admittedly, reality’s taken a downturn from the days when Tesla investors cheered on the potential policy advantages that’d favor FSD and robotaxi developments under the Trump administration. Trump’s earlier promises to prioritize a regulatory framework that’d assist the general availability of autonomous vehicle (“AV”) technologies have essentially received zero air-time since he took office.

But that’s not to say Tesla’s robotaxi and FSD ambitions are still wildcards.

To date, Tesla’s done and invested more than enough to call the two endeavors key components of its business model. FSD monetization’s made huge leaps since its early days – especially since the roll-out of FSD v12. This was further verified by positive user feedback and collective support shown for Tesla’s latest FSD technology, despite YouTuber Mark Rober’s recent “fake wall crash test” performed with a Tesla that has a form of its AV technology engaged. In the past, such adverse test results would’ve instead warranted immediate skepticism over the technology (similar to Xiaomi’s (OTCPK:XIACF) current crisis following the recent crash of a SU7 while its “smart driving software” was engaged). As a result, FSD contributions to Tesla’s fundamental outlook shouldn’t be dismissed. And neither should Tesla’s robotaxi prospects be diminished when gauging its valuation outlook, given the impending business unit’s an extension of FSD adoption.

Our downside scenario forecast projects FSD revenue to expand at a 53% 10-year CAGR, generated primarily from a combination of FSD license sales to individual car owners, FSD monthly subscriptions, and FSD technology licensing to OEMs. The downside scenario FSD monetization trajectory is projected as a function of downside scenario auto sales discussed in the earlier section, which captures the anticipated 25% downward shift to its longer-term growth curve due to impending demand risks.

By applying a 11x multiple on 2035 FSD sales projected for the downside scenario and discounted, the business unit yields an estimated intrinsic value of $193 billion, or $55 per share. The valuation multiple assumption applied is consistent with the average observed across high-growth software names, which exhibit a similar margin profile and scalability as FSD.

Meanwhile, robotaxi monetization is not expected to yield a material impact on Tesla until at least 2035. It’s likely that it’ll take at least another decade for Tesla’s FSD Unsupervised technology to become a global standard, which full-scale worldwide robotaxi roll-out is dependent on. Our projections expect robotaxi revenue to expand at a 4% five-year CAGR through 2040. Although the assumption appears conservative, we believe it appropriately captures the inherent execution risks facing robotaxis, especially in the downside scenario. Since commercialization at scale is still further out in the future, the business’ materialization outlook remains prone to ongoing regulatory hurdles, evolving consumer dynamics, and changes in the competitive landscape.

Similar to how we’ve valued Tesla’s FSD business, we’ve applied a 11x multiple on projected 2040 robotaxi sales in the downside scenario given similarities in their earnings profile and scalability. After discounting the figure to present value, Tesla’s robotaxi business yields a projected intrinsic value of $325 billion, or $92 per share.

Taken together, FSD and robotaxi collective represents $147 per share in our projected downside scenario price for Tesla. The two components, though not clearly evident in Tesla’s existing operations, essentially represent the non-traditional, yet critical pieces of the stock’s valuation, which shouldn’t be overlooked.

4. Don’t Miss the Impending Catalysts

Under the sum-of-the-parts valuation approach outlined in the foregoing analysis, Tesla should be worth at least $282 per share in the downside scenario. Yet, it’s currently trading at about $50 below our downside scenario price, which already factors a 25% downward shift to its forward growth trajectory to account for imminent demand risks. We believe this $50 gap is merely reflective of “noise” – which yields no tangible impact on underlying fundamentals that underpin Tesla’s cash flow and valuation prospects.

Fundamentally, we believe the back-of-the-napkin downside scenario calculation of Tesla’s valuation outlook based on the anticipated pace of economic deterioration remains intact. The 25% discount to base case NTM growth projections effectively captures the totality of impending sales and earnings volatility inflicted by tariffs, the global demand downturn, AV regulatory woes, and intensifying competition. As a result, the stock’s $50 discount at current levels in comparison to our downside scenario price target for Tesla reflects an attractive opportunity, in our opinion.

Beyond fundamentals, Tesla’s still got several levers up its sleeve that can trigger catalyst-driven upward re-rates to the stock. This has often been a key value appreciation driver for Tesla, which explains the volatile nature of the stock.

Specifically, the Tesla stock’s shown inherent sensitivity to headline shocks, especially with regard to updates on FSD and robotaxi developments. This was evident following Tesla’s “We, Robot” event in October, which accompanied the stock on a 101% upsurge to its December peak, despite only showcasing geo-fenced Cybercabs and backend-controlled humanoid robots.

As discussed in earlier sections, Musk’s symbolic return to Tesla operations post-DOGE would be a potential catalyst for the stock, as it’d assuage growing investor concerns about “Elon distraction.” This should coincide with improvements in uptake for the new Model Y (Launch Series in the U.S. and Juniper in China), the introduction of the “more affordable model,” and most importantly, the roll-out of FSD Unsupervised in June.

Specifically, the on-time roll-out of FSD Unsupervised in Texas in June should yield a positive inflection for the stock. The achievement would mark the completion of a key prerequisite for robotaxi roll-out that Musk’s currently penciled-in for late 2026 or early 2027. If this is accompanied by the resumed roll-out of FSD in China, then it’d only reinforce the robotaxi narrative that’s been driving Tesla’s valuation.

Conclusion

Taken together, Tesla looks well-positioned for a 2023 style recovery where it rose from the ashes following the “Twitter spiral.” Despite ongoing market uncertainties, we believe Tesla’s trading at a decent discount to its fundamental prospects in the downside scenario, which represents opportunity. With Musk’s DOGE responsibilities nearing an end, Tesla stock’s turnaround should be right around the corner.

data.title: RLX Technology: A Mispriced Leader With Potential For Rebound
data.description:
Summary

RLX Technology (NYSE: RLX) is both a growth and recovery story stock that is heavily mispriced. Despite strict crackdown by the Chinese government, I believe the market is still mispricing it as just a domestic recovery story, but it offers a good risk to reward ratio due to the story of international expansion from limited disclosure and strong net cash position plus underwhelming valuation.

Investment Thesis

RLX is one of the top leading e-cigarette companies in China. Its share price performance was penalized heavily, dropping ~92% since its all-time high due to China's 2021-2022 vaping crackdown. However, the market is still mispricing RLX even though there is clearer regulatory visibility ahead, new growth engines and improving profitability. Moreover, RLX has a huge net cash position, trading at deeper discount compared to its peers and its overseas expansion coming to fruition. I believe these catalysts would reprice RLX to a higher valuation. Ultimately, RLX presents an asymmetric risk-to-reward for patient investors.

E-Cigarette Industry Crackdown

From 2018 to 2021, there were many e-cigarette brands, such as RLX, MOTI and YOOZ, popping up and there was very little control over the sector development. It became very popular among the young people and many products were sold online. This became worrying due to its uncontrolled nature, and the Chinese government decided to classify e-cigarettes as a tobacco product. They cite reasons such as easy accessibility to youths, health concerns and the need for standardization. This means that vapes would be subjected to the same rules such as licensing, import/export, production, etc. Moreover, non-tobacco flavors are banned, and they are subjected to 36% ad valorem tax for production and import and 11% wholesale.

This means that both online distribution channels and product were swiftly disrupted, and many unlicensed and unprofitable players have to exit the industry, revamping the industry landscape to only a few big players left. Therefore, RLX was heavily impacted. It lost its profitable products and sales channels almost overnight. But this also creates an opportunity ahead as RLX was able to not just survive but also rebuilt its business into the top player in China.

Addressing Citi Downgrade

Citi analyst downgraded RLX from Buy to Neutral due to a shift towards as “Big Puff” disposables and potential regulatory tightening abroad. This means that there could be compression in average selling price as disposable vapes are getting more popular. However, these risks appear manageable and already reflected in the current valuation. Moreover, the firm actually beat analyst estimates for its Q4 earnings and the share price decline is unwarranted. It is mainly sentiment-based and not founded on fundamentals. Remember, RLX has secured its leadership in the Chinese market even with regulatory clamp-down. I believe even though ASP would be lesser, it still allows RLX to enter into Southeast Asia and Latin America markets with flavor products. With a strong cash position, and optionality in multiple geographies, RLX is able to weather these challenges.

Financials

RLX on a financial basis is doing pretty well. Even though it's from a low base, the FY24 Q4 revenue is growing at 56% YoY, increasing by the quarters. This is a reflection of a business growing back to its former glory. Its balance sheet is also very clean. The cash balances exceed its total liabilities. We are essentially buying the "company for free". It has $1.1bn of cash and just $120mil worth of liabilities. It is essentially a special situation investing. Lastly, its cash flow has been positive year-on-year and free cash flow has also grown positively. I believe this is a good sign and the company is showing really strong numbers.

Catalysts to Unlock Upside

I believe there are multiple catalysts that could push higher earnings and unlock its value.

Stabilizing Regulatory Policies

China’s new e-cigarette regulatory framework administered by the State Tobacco Monopoly Administration (STMA) is now in place. All vape makers have to be licensed and sell only tobacco-flavored products under strict standardization. The Chinese Government probably would probably not do a total industry ban as it would be leaving out the tax which would serve as additional tax revenue for the Chinese government and also what they had set out to do, which is control, has already been accomplished.

Although RLX's revenue is ~30% impacted in 2022 due to the new manufacturing license, there is still a silver lining among this turbulence. There are enforcements towards illegal competitors which siphon demand away from licensed players. As this goes on, the demand would be re-directed to RLX overtime. I think this is not yet fully priced in as more illegal sites are clamped, RLX would benefit from it overtime.

International Expansion Unappreciated

I believe RLX's international expansion is highly underrated. RLX acquired three overseas e-vapor businesses from Southeast Asia and North Asia in late 2023. These acquisitions have transformed RLX into a Pan-Asia company. Even though the acquisition was in 2023, RLX’s international growth is not fully reflected in its valuation.

Its RMB 229 million overseas revenue figure is also backward-looking, as this figure only reflects the late-2023 acquisitions. The company did not disclose in its filings by quarter, region, or margin. Therefore, I think analyst and investors do not have the necessary data to model out the overseas growth, which means the market is not able to fully appreciate it. Furthermore, I can assume that the international products have higher margins due to its lesser regulations, which also means they could sell higher margin flavor vapes. Therefore, RLX is priced as a China-recovery stock but not as a growth stock entering international markets.

In fact, it is quietly emerging to be an international e-vaping player. Once the management has disclosed its international revenue inside its filings, the information gap should close, and the market would re-rate the stock upwards.

Therefore, for the next 2–3 quarters, once international revenue becomes a solid growth engine, there could be more clarity in the disclosures, and continued enforcement against unlicensed players in China, which would drive sentiment shifts or positive revisions to the share price.

Risk

Whilst the policy measures are behind us, the Chinese government could still revert and limit RLX's revenue stream and adjust nicotine limits. International markets may also follow suit and call for a control towards e-cigarette ban. However, like I mentioned earlier, I believe this would be unlikely as the government already has a control over the sector. Another risk is poor integration. If new markets fail to scale successfully, this will lead to higher cost and compression in margins. However, I think the attractive valuation justify taking the risk.

Valuation & Scenario Analysis

RLX’s current valuation is compelling: ~50% of its market cap are cash, and its stock is trading lower than its peers. I believe RLX is an undervalued machine that is poised for re-rating. Its Chinese peers are private, so I would be looking at its international counterparts for comparison. For example, Philip Morris is trading at 33x Forward P/E, and Turning Point Brands is trading at 18x Forward P/E and RLX is trading at 15x forward P/E. Thus, it is undervalued on a relative basis.

Bear Case: Assuming the China market is stagnant, and its overseas revenue contribute modestly, RLX’s revenue would grow to $370M which is a 10% revenue growth on FY2025. I chose 10% growth as further ASP compression due to selling disposable vapes, stagnant Chinese market, and it is also lower than sell-side consensus screenshot above. With net margins maintaining at ~24%, RLX could generate around ~ $90M in net income. Assuming its P/E is around 12× for conservative purpose (trailing P/E is around 30x), its implied operating business valuation is around $1.1bn and adding it its $1.1bn of net cash, the total business should roughly be worth $2.2bn​. Thus, its huge net cash position contributes to a wide downside protection, contributing to a 4% downside with these assumptions.

Do also note that from FY22-23, revenue dropped significantly from $766M to $175M due to the vaping crackdown and that has already been priced in and from what I mentioned above, I don't think this would repeat again as the tobacco industry is an important sector that contributes to the government tax revenue and the control that the Chinese government want has already been accomplished. In fact, in 2022 the tax revenue is comparable to China's defense budget of $213bn. Thus, this shows that it is an important revenue stream for the government.

Base Case: I assume continued recovery due to demand returning back to licensed players and foreign markets growing successfully. By FY2025, RLX’s revenues could approach ~$400M, growing at 20%. Yes, this is above sell-side consensus, but I think 20% is not over the top as domestic recovery and overseas expansion should drive revenue to that level easily and also pre-crackdown revenue growth was at triple digit % growth. Net margins might expand a bit to 26% due to economics of scale, and net income could grow to about $100M. Giving it a multiple P/E of 20× which is still below its trailing P/E and adding back net cash of $1.1bn gives me an implied valuation of $3.1bn with ~34% upside.

Bull Case: I assume China’s vaping market grows back to high growth due to loosening regulation and higher demand and successful overseas expansion. With this, I expect revenue to grow to $450M by FY2025 which is 30% growth. With a 26% net margin from economics of scale and cost optimization, net income would be at $116M. The market might even award a 25x P/E. Philip Morris trades ~20×, and high-growth consumer firms can be around 20–30×. Thus, 25x is not impossible to think of. Adding net cash, the total business could be valued at $4.1bn, effectively an upside of 78%.

Conclusion

Therefore, the scenarios do seem to show that there is an asymmetric set-up that is favored towards the upside. Given RLX's misunderstood growth drivers and cash-rich positioning, I think adding at this price would be good as there is downside protection and if you're looking for a high contrarian set-up, RLX is the way to go.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Keep Calm And Dividend On: 3 Future Proof Stocks To Buy Amid Market Chaos
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

May you live in interesting times. Unprecedented once in a century tariffs have ruffled the market's feathers -- to say the least.

The S&P 500 (SPX) had one of its worst losing streaks ever, as markets didn't react too well to the administration's enacting of across the board tariffs on goods coming into the US.

I've been pretty unfazed about it. Here's my take: Does it cause a problem for the economy? Yes, in the short term there is disruption. Does it cause real trouble for the markets? No, not really, the financial conditions are lining up for more liquidity to come onto the markets, which should support this bull market further.

In this article, I'll talk about the current disruption in markets, what I believe is driving confusion, and then offer 3 dividend stocks which, I believe, have gotten unfairly bashed in the correction, yet stand to bounce back aggressively.

Curiouser and Curiouser!

In Lewis Carroll's classic book, Alice and Wonderland, Alice drinks from a bottle labeled "Drink me". It causes her to shrink. She later eats a cake that scales her to a huge size.

These large and confusing signs cause her to cry: Curiouser and Curioser!

The scale of market movements over the past few weeks reflect this, as the market has corrected in a near perfect "expanding triangle" structure, which is common in markets where confusion is rampant, as bulls and bears battle it out.

Where could this confusion come from?

I'm going to have to ask you, dear reader, to set your political preferences aside for a minute.

I have mine, you have yours, if we talked it out over a drink, I'm sure you'll find that we have more in common than we are different.

Among our commonalities, is the fact that we both want more wealth. Why else would you be taking from your precious time on earth to read a publication called "Seeking Alpha"?

The fact of the matter is that in the US, depending on your party allegiance, your outlook of the economy is going to be skewed.

An overwhelming number of democrats view economic conditions as worsening (an estimated 93.5% if we interpret the data below as being calculated from an either-or question).

A vast majority of Republicans view economic conditions as getting better (77.5%, based on the numbers presented, under the same assumption as above).

Are Democrats and Republicans getting fundamentally different data? Or are they basing their decisions based on emotions, which derives from the content that they consume?

We already know that content individuals consume on social media platforms is tailored towards their political preferences, and therefore bias-ing clear-headed thinking.

Media outlets also lean one way or another (i.e. the New York Time and Washington Post have a left bias, Fox has a right bias).

This, most Americans agree on, according to research from Pew Research.

So, is it possible that market participants are also emotional human beings with political preferences, content preferences, and that the content they are consuming is impacting their worldview and therefore their market views?

This thesis would align very closely with the way the current market is whipsawing. We are living in Dicken's "tale of two cities":

It's the best of times, it's the worst of times. Just depends on who you ask.

What I suggest today, is that we look beyond the headlines, at the data.

And I confess that I certainly have my own biases, and I'll let you decide which way they might influence my writing.

It's all going to be all right in the end

At the entrance of a new-wave hippie cafe in Bali, which my wife dragged me to, there was a sign: "It's all going to be all right in the end, if it's not all right, it's not the end."

And I believe this applies perfectly to investing in equities in the US.

US equities have been the gift that keeps giving for over a century, and structurally there is no indication that this will change in the future.

The US is at the cutting edge of AI technology and its implementations, and is by far its largest beneficiary.

So why would a government decide to upend the world order by creating a trade wall around its country? Maybe to force manufacturing to come back to the US. Is it for jobs? Not directly, no. Modern factories require nowhere near as many workers as they did when those factories left the US. Soon they'll need even less.

If we look out maximum 10 years from now, it seems very unlikely that our factories are not going to be robots with AI. It's the path of least resistance and highest profitability, so usually it's the path down which we will go.

Whoever gets to scale AI robots building the things we consume first, wins. If supply chains aren't already geared towards domestic manufacturing, then the race will be lost. Strategically over a multi-year perspective, this makes a lot of sense, even if it means short-term pain.

Our whole culture has become so dead set on instant gratification and pleasure that the idea of sacrifice seems unfathomable. But taking a short-term dislocation for a long-term advantage is the fundamental concept of winning at capitalism.

Long term, it will all be all right. Even though it is very possible that the US is in a recession as we speak.

And short term, what do we think of the consequences?

I believe the current administration is not getting enough credit. Again, I do not align with all their policy (not a political wholesaler), but I believe that, for instance, Scott Bessent, who is now secretary of Treasury after a career in global macro hedge funds, might just know a thing or two about macro.

Let's take stock of what is happening.

Sure, the S&P 500 is down around 18% from its ATH.

Then the 10-year yield is now down 70 bips without the Fed having moved rates or even signaled further rate cuts.

And the market now expects that it is more likely that rates are cut at the June 18th meeting than not, with the probability of a 25bp Fed Fund rate cut in June now at 64% vs. 44% when Trump came into the office.

The dollar index has declined by 6% since Trump's inauguration.

How do you bring all these metrics together?

The 10-year yield is the benchmark for borrowing rates throughout the entire economy. A lower 10 year allows the Treasury to refinance debt at a lower rate. This is, of course, of particular interest in a year in which some $7 trillion of debt needs to be refinanced.

Furthermore, lower US yields make credit cheaper everywhere, as emerging market corporations often borrow in dollars (there's little appetite for foreign currencies in many cases) so it fuels liquidity globally. Lower yields push investors out of cash and treasuries and into riskier assets, which increases liquidity in circulation among these assets.

Next, if the Fed is compelled to lower its target rates, this directly impacts short-term borrowing for consumers and businesses, and increases the opportunity of keeping cash, which stimulates spending and investment into higher yielding assets and ventures, stimulating the economy.

With the yield curve now uninverted (except for the very short term 0-1 year yields), we can expect rate cuts to add extra pressure on the entire yield curve.

Finally, a lower dollar makes US debt cheaper to foreign investors, which stimulates demand for US treasuries all else held equal, and increases foreign capital flows. Foreign investors own about 1/3rd of US debt, so this cannot be ignored.

This makes US denominated assets easier to service, which frees up liquidity, which then leads to more money supply globally.

The consequence of this trifecta is easing financial conditions, which in turn stimulates the economy, which gives me confidence that any recession would be short-lived.

So we can all look at the headlines we chose to read, or we can look at the data.

The data tells me, we will be all right.

Especially for longer term oriented dividend investors like ourselves, who practice a "buy low, sell high, get paid to wait" approach.

Here are 3 stocks which, I believe, will be future-proof and are now unfairly cheap amid the recent decline

3 future-proof stocks.

Those who look beyond the headlines and short-term FUD, will inevitably be faced with the reality that we stand on the threshold of an economy reshaped by artificial intelligence and electrification. Onshoring of manufacturing is just one part of that puzzle.

Who will be key players in these verticals over the next 10 years? Sure, we can look at the obvious tech pundits, but I'll offer you to look beyond that at some of the necessary bits and pieces that will hold it altogether.

1. NEE: The Renewable Powerhouse.

NextEra Energy (NEE) provides essential renewable energy, which will be in ever more demand over upcoming years.

I've argued in the past that Trump's presidency will be bullish for clean energy.

I said:

Ushering in a golden age of American innovation might require deregulation, but it will also require investment across the whole spectrum of the energy mix, something that the market is not fully appreciating, despite it being written in plain text in the GOP's platform.

You can read all about it here.

NEE anticipates an increase of 55% in power demand over the next two decades.

The stock currently trades at $66 and yields 3.48%, well above its 10-year median of 2.59%.

During the past decade, the dividend has grown at 11.4% per year, 10% over the past 1 and 5 years. I continue to see 8-12% dividend growth throughout the rest of the decade, as this best in class utility continues to execute.

In a few years, you'll either look back happy with your NEE stock, or unable to believe you missed out on what is setting up to be a double over the next 3-5 years.

2. Brookfield Asset Management: The Infrastructure Investor

Ok, so critical infrastructure needs to be built out: data centers, solar and wind projects, battery storage projects, new manufacturing facilities.

These projects need to be financed and managed, and this is where Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) comes into play.

This alt investor manager with $1tn in assets is actively participating in all the major verticals that support AI technologies.

The company is brilliantly managed, they have created immense shareholder wealth and will continue to do so. The DFT chart above doesn't fully serve it, as the post 2023 company is different than before. We wrote for members of the Dividend Freedom Tribe an in-depth dive looking at all of Brookfield's associated entities and different investment companies with those which we like, and explaining why BAM is our favorite.

One thing is unquestionable: It is clearly mispriced. Today, at $44, BAM yields 4%. That's with a dividend which grew 15% this year, and at a 20% CAGR over the past decade.

I think we can clearly expect 10-20% dividend growth per year (not giving too precise a prediction here, I concede) over upcoming years, which combined with a 4% yield is just a killer proposition.

It's one of my favorite stocks, period.

3. Goldman Sachs: The Strategic Advisor

Finally, it follows that overpriced help, as Warren Buffett called them, will always be needed in facilitating deals, underwriting equity and debt operations.

Enter Goldman Sachs (GS), which has zipped from $668 to $465 over the course of the past two months, creating a brilliant opportunity for investors to enter at the current price.

The dividend only increased by 9% last year, well below the 10 year CAGR of 17%, but this, I think, is more of a bug than a feature, and we'll continue to see GS grow at 10-12% over upcoming years, as it continues to be the best-in-class investment bank out there.

Today GS yields 2.58%, above its 10-year median yield of 1.85% and offers a compelling opportunity to investors.

The DFT chart shows that since 2021, the 1.85% yield level (separation between light blue and pink) has been a roof for GS. I don't think this will last.

I think as markets turn around, GS will zip back up, and we once again have a setup for a double over the next 3 years.

Buy the best at a great price, why overthink it?

Conclusion: If you have a big problem, you're being a small person

How big we perceive problems to be is usually a function of our ability to handle them.

The first time you are faced with a problem, it might seem big. The 200th time it is no longer a problem, it is just something you have to deal with.

The nature of the problem didn't change, you did.

It's the same with market correction. In my young age I've lived through a handful now, and studied every single one which I was too young or unborn to analyze.

Optimism, level-headed thinking, and being steady on the tiller are attributes which lead to success.

Apply them to investing and you'll do all-right.

data.title: Why The Market Is Wrong On Nvidia: Initiating The Stock With A Buy Under $100
data.description:
I’m initiating Wall Street’s darling Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) with a buy. If you bought at market open, you got the stock at $89, and that’s a steal, so congrats. While a market crash can prove scary to some, I’m buying the panic. And with a company like Nvidia, a buy case isn’t really that complicated to make, but Nvidia under $100 makes an even easier case.

Nvidia under a $100 is a rare occurrence, and if we’re taking the split into account, the last time the stock was trading below a $100 was back in August last year.

The tariff situation

I’m not too worried about Nvidia in regards to tariffs in the short term, and I share CEO Jensen Huang’s sentiment on that. The company is likely exempt from the latest tariff announcements under the USMCA. According to trade data, Nvidia’s U.S. AI server shipments come in largely from Mexico (roughly 60%) and another 30% from Taiwan. So the dilemma going forward is: will Nvidia be able to maintain its GPU pricing power?

Following President Trump’s tariff announcement last week, Nvidia and TSMC (TSM) both fell over 5%, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) down 4%, and Broadcom (AVGO) down another 7%, while Nvidia’s key memory chip supplier Micron Technology (MU) was down 7%. Trump’s 54% total tariffs on China and 32% reciprocal tariffs on Taiwan sent the market into a shock. TSMC, which produces chips for Nvidia, AMD, and Intel (INTC) are at the heart of it all; for some context, the U.S. imported around $19 billion in servers equipped with Nvidia GPUs from Taiwan and $34 billion from China in 2024. Trump thinks the tariffs should bring manufacturing back to the U.S., as he points at TSMC’s $100 billion investment in Arizona, which started way back under the Biden Administration.

Still, the uncertainty around the tariff situation isn’t to be ignored. With the U.S. threatening an additional 50% tariff on China starting Wednesday unless China takes back its 34% reciprocal tariff on the U.S., tariffs on China could total a staggering 104%.

According to the company’s 10-K and 10-Q, revenue from sales to customers outside the U.S. decreased from 69% in the fiscal year 2023 to 56% in the fiscal year 2024 and 53% in the fiscal year 2025 due to “higher U.S.-based Compute & Networking segment demand.” The U.S. accounted for around 46.9% in revenue for FY25, up from around 44.2% in FY24 and around 30.7% in FY23. So while this is a more balanced exposure, the company still makes a significant amount of its sales from the rest of the world and that means if other nations like Singapore or Taiwan place tariffs on the U.S. in retaliation to Trump’s Liberation Day, then Nvidia could be looking at trouble.

The U.S. now makes nearly half of Nvidia’s revenue, as seen below. Exposure to Singapore and Taiwan is still relevant. but the latter is shrinking to around 16% in FY25, it’s down from 22% in FY24 and 25% in FY23.

I think we’re likely to see this from countries other than China because what we’ve seen so far indicates that Trump wants to cut deals and negotiate, and it looks like that’s a more likely scenario, or an eventual scenario, over a back and forth trade war between the U.S. and everyone else.

When it comes to China, I like that Nvidia is reducing its exposure to China, with revenue from China down from 21.5% in FY23 to around 13.1% in FY25, cushioning the company against U.S. chip restrictions. All in all, I think Nvidia is better positioned to maneuver the effects of the tariffs and remain a leader in the space in the long term.

Valuation and price movement:

The stock was down over 7.9% to $86 per share at market open on Monday, but rebounded rather quickly back to $94, and closed over 3.5% higher at $97.6. There is still a buy-on-the-dip opportunity here, with the stock down over 13.3% over the past 5 days, down another 16% on the one-month chart, and a staggering 31% year to date. I don’t see much downside ahead.

The stock is currently trading under its EMA21 at $111.2, EMA50 at $118.9, and EMA200 at $119.5, as seen below.

RSI was at around 32, nearing oversold territory at the beginning of the trading day on Monday, but has since recovered slightly and currently stands at 33, signaling bullish momentum.

The valuation metrics also look attractive at current levels in comparison to its peers and its past. Nvidia’s forward P/E at 20.8, slightly higher than AMD's at 18.3, and significantly lower than Intel's at 40. Nvidia’s forward PEG ratio is currently at 0.59, below AMD at 0.63, and the sector median at 1.29.

Bullish Investment Thesis:

Nvidia is an AI leader, and no one has come close, with competitors like Advanced Micro and Intel struggling to take market share. I’m optimistic about Nvidia for the long term and think the company is ahead of competitors and the market alike. I see some volatility in the short term, with all the tariff uncertainty, but I think this is a good time to start buying in bunches.

The company led the data center GPU market and held a 92% market share, a step closer to total dominance. Through 2024, the company’s quarterly revenue was on the rise, with a record annual revenue of over $115 billion in 2024, up a staggering 142% year over year. Nvidia’s strategy is to be the all-in-one supplier of AI data centers, and continuous software innovations (and all the time spent working on improving CUDA, which is currently the company’s biggest moat) have helped maintain the crown, not to mention the most recent Blackwell-architecture GPUs which have become the new standard for data center GPUs.

Going forward, I’m not only optimistic about Nvidia for being a leader in the GPU market, but also other markets it could potentially be a leader in like the data center networking market and the PC client market. On the former, the question now is: who can deliver switches with higher bandwidth and lower latency?

Nvidia is now trying to create a new standard by “integrating silicon photonics directly into switches, Nvidia is shattering the old limitations of hyperscale and enterprise networks and opening the gate to million-GPU AI factories,” according to the company’s CEO Jensen Huang. At GTC 2025, Nvidia introduced its Spectrum-X Photonics (to arrive in 2026) and Quantum-X Photonics (to be released in 2025) networking switch platforms, which up data transfer speed to 1.6 Tb/s per port (twice the maximum of current top-tier copper Ethernet solutions, and 400 Tb/s in the aggregate), which allows millions of GPUs to operate together rather seamlessly. According to the company, the new switches offer higher bandwidth and lower power loss. In comparison to Broadcom’s open networking environment, which allows data centers to integrate components from various vendors, Nvidia is an advocate for a vertically integrated solution, combining both its hardware and software for optimized performance.

The data center networking market is expected to grow from $37.6 billion in 2024 to $64.2 billion by 2029, at a CAGR of 11.3% with AI and ML integrations projected to boost market demand.

Nvidia dominated the GPU market, and they have the client base, so who’s to say they can’t do it again? I remain optimistic about Nvidia long-term and think the stock's current price is an attractive entry point for investors.

data.title: Utilities Sector Update - Competitive Growth Along With Above-Average Price Stability And Attractive Dividend Yields
data.description:
Our last blog discussed safe harbors amidst the current storm of market volatility. The idea presented was to investigate the strong buy stocks with the lowest betas. This week, we home in on a sector that is typically low volatility and low growth. Currently, we find fairly competitive earnings growth in this sector. We look in detail and compare 6 Utilities stocks, and 7 Utility Sector ETFs in this post.

The Utilities industry in the United States is a cornerstone of economic stability and growth, providing essential services such as electricity, gas, and water to millions of consumers. This report focuses on six notable US domiciled stocks in the Utilities sector, identified by their ticker symbols: XEL, D, NEE, UGI, AEP, and AWK. All are rated as 5 (Strong Buy) by ValuEngine’s predictive model.

I include a brief synopsis of the predictive model’s methodology for the uninitiated. The model uses proprietary algorithms that incorporate financial metrics such as earnings, forward estimates, interest rate forecasts, revenue growth, profit margins, and return on equity to assess stock performance. The methodology emphasizes fundamental analysis, examining key financial statements, industry trends, and company-specific factors to determine intrinsic value. Stocks are compared within their industry peers. ValuEngine also factors in risk assessment, considers market volatility, economic conditions, and company-specific risks to provide a comprehensive evaluation. This allows us to evaluate relative performance and identify potential investment opportunities. Those relative opportunities are characterized by our ratings which range from 1 (Strong Sell) to 5 (Strong Buy).

We take a bottom-up approach to sector evaluation to create our Sector Report. Besides low beta and high dividend yield, another reason the Utilities Sector was chosen to be featured today is that it is currently the third highest sector in terms of current investment opportunity with a rating of 3.5 out of a possible 5. I note that historically the utilities sector is for income investors more than for investors looking for price appreciation. Because of this, the sector has commonly been in the bottom half of forecast price gains. There are a number of fundamental underpinnings of the industry which explains the unusual opportunities to derive income, superior return/risk ratios and top-quartile price gains from the sector.

The Utilities industry sector is undergoing a transformative phase characterized by several key trends:

  • A significant shift towards renewable energy sources such as wind, solar, and hydroelectric power is reshaping the Utilities sector. This transition is driven by both regulatory mandates and consumer demand for cleaner energy.
  • The adoption of smart grid technology, enhanced battery storage, and energy management systems is optimizing efficiency and reliability in energy distribution.
  • Stringent environmental regulations and policies are compelling utilities to reduce carbon emissions and invest in sustainable infrastructure.
  • There is a considerable focus on upgrading aging infrastructure to enhance resilience against natural disasters and improve service reliability.

The six Strong Buy 5-rated companies under consideration, XEL, D, NEE, UGI, AEP, and AWK, are well-positioned to capitalize on these trends. In fact, excepting AWK, the rest can be thought of as energy technology companies.

Xcel Energy (XEL) is a leader in the renewable energy transition, with substantial investments in wind and solar power. The company’s commitment to reducing carbon emissions and its innovative solutions make it a key player in the future of energy.

Dominion Energy (D) is at the forefront of infrastructure modernization, with significant projects aimed at enhancing grid reliability and expanding renewable energy capacity. The company’s strategic initiatives align technological trends.

NextEra Energy (NEE) is renowned for its aggressive expansion into renewable energy, particularly wind and solar power. Its extensive portfolio of clean energy projects positions it as a frontrunner in the Utilities sector’s transformation.

UGI Corporation (UGI) focuses on natural gas distribution and energy services, with an emphasis on sustainability and energy efficiency. The company’s diverse operations and commitment to innovation provide a solid foundation for growth.

American Electric Power (AEP) is heavily invested in upgrading its transmission infrastructure and expanding its renewable energy footprint. AEP’s initiatives are geared towards enhancing grid resilience and meeting evolving energy needs.

American Water Works (AWK) is the largest publicly traded water and wastewater utility company in the US. Its focus on infrastructure investment and regulatory compliance ensures the provision of high-quality water services.

The last three rows, analyzed in the aggregate, bolsters the investment thesis that the past 12 months has provided unusual opportunities for price appreciation for utilities. All six utilities standard-bearers have outgained the S&P 500 ETF during the past 12 months. Only NextEra, the only one of the six to have a negative return thus far in 2025, has underperformed on a Year-to-Date basis. It is not surprising at all that all six stocks have dividend yields considerably higher than SPLG. Dominion Energy’s 5.0% dividend yield is the highest of the group. The returns forecast by the ValuEngine model follows the same pattern as what has been realized during the past 12 months. The projected utility stock returns range from 14.1% to 16.7% as compared with just 4.9% expected from the S&P 500 ETF. Although all six utility stocks are rated strong buys, Xcel Energy (XEL) and Dominion Energy (D) are the two most attractive now. Another plus for D is having an earnings growth rate of 23.6%, the highest in the study.

A similar picture is painted when moving on to ETFs. Given the unusual opportunities to invest for growth as well as income in the Utilities Sector and six of its largest stocks, it may not be as surprising as it would have been normally to find that the three largest Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) in the Utilities sector also are rated 5 (Strong Buy). As most regular readers of this blog probably know, I was an early pioneer of ETFs and generally recommend most investors that don’t consider themselves traders buy and hold ETFs in lieu of individual stocks, ETFs offer diversified exposure to the industry with very low expense ratios, Accordingly, they have become the tools most commonly used to implement sector rotation strategies in attempts to take advantage of cyclical returns and risk-taking changes in the markets. The six largest Utilities sector ETFs are:

Utility Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU) is the largest and most popular Utilities sector ETFs, with substantial assets under management. It tracks the performance of the Utilities Select Sector Index, a market-cap-weighted index comprising a broad range of utility companies.

Vanguard Utilities ETF (VPU) follows the performance of the MSCI US Investable Market Utilities 25/50 Index, a larger and more diverse utilities index, but is still market-cap weighted

Fidelity MSCI Utilities Index ETF (FUTY) tracks the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Utilities Index, providing broad exposure to the Utilities sector. Its holdings include a mix of electricity, gas, and water utilities. FUTY has the lowest expense ratio of the six ETFs at 0.08% (8 basis points, 1 basis point lower than XLU and VP.

iShares Utilities ETF (IDU) tracks the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Utilities Index, providing broad exposure to the Utilities sector. Its holdings include a mix of electricity, gas, and water utilities. Its expense ratio is at least 30 points higher than that of the above three ETFs without attempting to find excess alpha or other value added.

First Trust Utilities AlphaDEX® Fund (FXU) seeks investment results that correspond generally to the price and yield, before fees and expenses, of an equity index called the StrataQuant® Utilities Index. This proprietary strategy index uses fundamental data relating to earnings strength and valuation to select and weigh the portfolio constituents. It has the highest expense ratio of the six ETFs at a whopping 0.64% (64 basis points). Since it is value-added, the implicit aspiration is that FXU will deliver better performance that more than pays for the fee.

Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (UTES) aims to provide total return through a combination of capital appreciation and income, primarily by investing in equity securities of companies in the utility sector, and is actively managed, not seeking to replicate a specific index. Once again, SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG) is used for comparisons.
